precedents.summary: Applicant reported significant deterioration in mobility following hip replacement complications. GP report confirmed walking distance reduced to under 20 metres. Physiotherapy assessment supported the applicant's account. Original standard rate mobility revised to enhanced rate.
keyEvidence: Physiotherapy assessment with measured walking distance
keyFactors: Measured walking distance under 20 metres was decisive
Multiple medical sources corroborated the applicant's account
Deterioration was clearly evidenced since the original award

precedents.summary: Applicant disputed the refusal of a mobility component, reporting difficulty walking due to knee pain. GP letter confirmed diagnosis but did not quantify functional limitation. No physiotherapy or specialist assessment was available. The evidence was insufficient to demonstrate that the applicant met the descriptor thresholds for either planning journeys or moving around.
keyEvidence: Applicant's own account on PIP2 form
keyFactors: GP letter confirmed diagnosis but did not address functional impact
No objective measurement of walking distance or mobility limitation
Applicant's own account alone was insufficient without corroborating medical evidence

precedents.summary: Scheduled review of enhanced rate daily living award. Occupational therapy assessment confirmed the applicant's needs remained stable. The applicant continued to require assistance with preparing food, managing medication, and washing. No change in circumstances since the original award.
keyFactors: OT assessment confirmed needs were stable since original award
Care plan corroborated the level of support required
No evidence of improvement or deterioration

precedents.reviewGrounds: Mandatory reconsideration — daily living and mobility
precedents.summary: Applicant disputed the absence of a mobility component alongside their standard rate daily living award. A rheumatology report confirmed significant joint pain affecting walking distance. The daily living component was maintained at standard rate. Standard rate mobility was added based on the rheumatology evidence showing the applicant could not walk more than 50 metres without significant pain.
keyFactors: Specialist rheumatology report was the decisive evidence for mobility
Walking distance quantified at under 50 metres with significant pain
Daily living component unchanged as no new evidence for that component

precedents.summary: Applicant disputed a fit-for-work decision. Psychiatrist report confirmed severe anxiety disorder with agoraphobia, preventing the applicant from leaving home unaccompanied. Employer sickness record showed 60 days absence in 6 months. The evidence demonstrated the applicant met the support group descriptor for substantial risk if found fit for work.
keyFactors: Psychiatrist report confirmed severity met support group threshold
Substantial risk descriptor was met — risk of significant deterioration if required to attend work-related activity
Employer sickness record corroborated the reported functional limitations

precedents.summary: Applicant disputed a fit-for-work decision citing depression and chronic fatigue. GP report confirmed diagnoses but noted the applicant was engaging with treatment and showing some improvement. The evidence supported limited capability for work (scoring 18 points across coping with change, social engagement, and initiating personal action) but did not meet the support group threshold.
keyFactors: Points scored across three mental health descriptors totalled 18
Treatment engagement suggested capability could improve
Support group threshold not met as no single descriptor was fully satisfied

precedents.summary: Applicant disputed a fit-for-work decision citing back pain. GP letter confirmed chronic lower back pain but noted the applicant was managing daily activities independently. No specialist assessment was provided. The applicant scored 9 points, below the 15-point threshold for limited capability for work.
keyFactors: GP evidence indicated the applicant was managing independently
Total points score of 9 was below the 15-point threshold
No specialist or occupational health evidence to support higher scoring

precedents.benefitType: Universal Credit — Limited capability for work
precedents.outcome: Revised — limited capability for work-related activity
precedents.summary: Applicant disputed a WCA outcome that found limited capability for work but not for work-related activity. A specialist pain consultant report confirmed fibromyalgia with severe fatigue episodes. The evidence demonstrated the applicant met the support group descriptor for substantial risk, as attempting work-related activity would cause significant deterioration in their condition.
keyFactors: Specialist pain report was decisive for the support group descriptor
Substantial risk of deterioration if required to undertake work-related activity
Occupational health assessment corroborated the functional limitations

precedents.summary: Applicant was awarded lower rate Attendance Allowance and disputed the refusal of the higher rate. A district nurse care log documented night-time visits 3 times per week for catheter care and medication administration. The care log demonstrated prolonged and repeated attention at night, meeting the night condition for the higher rate.
keyEvidence: District nurse care log covering 3 months
GP letter confirming night-time care needs
keyFactors: District nurse care log provided objective evidence of night-time attention
Three visits per week constituted 'repeated attention at night'
GP letter corroborated the need for night-time supervision

precedents.summary: Applicant disputed the refusal of higher rate Attendance Allowance, claiming night-time supervision needs. The GP letter confirmed the diagnosis but did not specifically address night-time care. No district nurse or carer evidence was provided. The applicant's own account described occasional night-time disturbance but not prolonged or repeated attention. The evidence did not meet the night condition threshold.
keyFactors: No objective evidence of night-time care from a professional carer or nurse
GP letter did not address night-time needs specifically
Applicant's account described occasional disturbance, not prolonged or repeated attention
